feedback.text: While the proposed measures are a step forward, they overlook the critical issue of consumer ownership in digital purchases. When buying digital goods like movies, music, software, and video games, consumers often assume they own the product. In reality, they only purchase a license, leaving them at the mercy of game publishers, streaming services, and other providers. This issue is especially problematic in the video game industry. Many modern games not only require online connectivity but also offer microtransactions or other means of payment, with some players spending significant amounts of money as part of their hobby. Despite these investments, publishers can revoke access to purchased digital goods or even prevent players from accessing full-price games once servers are shut down - a fate that has already befallen thousands of games. To address this, the Digital Fairness Act should mandate end-of-life plans from developers, ensuring continued access to purchased content through offline versions, private servers, or released source code. This would help secure true ownership and long-term access for consumers.
